title: Batching & SGD — Stochastic Noise as Feature
summary: Three-layer model: parallelism is the engineering reason for batching, SGD is the statistical license, gradient noise is the optimization feature (regularization, escape bad minima, generalization).
key_insights: SGD = statistical reason batching is valid (unbiased gradient estimate)
Gradient noise = optimization feature, not side effect — regularizer, prevents sharp minima
Full-batch GD gets stuck in sharp valleys; SGD noise finds broad, stable basins
Very large batches hurt generalization — noise reduction removes regularization
Analogy: SGD = noisy compass in fog — less precise but avoids traps

mathematical_core.full_gradient: ∇L(θ) = (1/N) Σᵢ ∇ℓ(θ, xᵢ) — average over ALL N training examples
mathematical_core.mini_batch_gradient: g_B(θ) = (1/|B|) Σᵢ∈B ∇ℓ(θ, xᵢ) — average over batch B ⊂ {1..N}
mathematical_core.unbiasedness: E[g_B(θ)] = ∇L(θ) — mini-batch gradient is unbiased estimate of full gradient
mathematical_core.variance: Var(g_B) = Var(∇ℓ) / |B| — variance decreases with batch size

derivations.title: Gradient noise as implicit regularization

steps.name: Noise structure
steps.math: g_B = ∇L + ε, where ε is zero-mean noise with Var(ε) = σ²/|B|. σ² = variance of per-sample gradients.

steps.name: Effect on optimization
steps.math: Update: θ_{t+1} = θ_t - η(∇L + ε_t). This is equivalent to gradient descent on a SMOOTHED loss landscape: the noise averages over nearby loss values, making sharp minima less attractive.

steps.name: Sharp vs flat minima
steps.math: Sharp minimum: high curvature, loss increases rapidly when θ is perturbed. SGD noise perturbs θ every step → model escapes sharp minima. Flat minimum: low curvature, loss changes slowly under perturbation. SGD noise doesn't push model out → model stays.

steps.name: Generalization connection
steps.math: Flat minima generalize better (small parameter perturbations ≈ distribution shift between train and test). Sharp minima overfit (sensitive to exact training data). SGD noise biases toward flat minima → better generalization.

derivations.consequence: SGD noise is a built-in regularizer that favors solutions that generalize. Removing it (large batch, low LR) often hurts test performance even if training loss is lower.

derivations.title: Batch size — learning rate scaling law

steps.name: Linear scaling rule
steps.math: If you multiply batch size by k, multiply learning rate by k to maintain the same effective update magnitude. Gradient variance decreases by k, so larger LR compensates.

steps.name: Breakdown at large batch
steps.math: Linear scaling works up to a critical batch size B_crit. Beyond B_crit, increasing batch size gives diminishing returns — gradient noise is already negligible and adding more samples doesn't help.

steps.name: Critical batch size
steps.math: B_crit ≈ B_noise / ε², where B_noise is the 'gradient noise scale' and ε is the target gradient accuracy. Empirically: B_crit grows with model size.

derivations.consequence: There's an optimal batch size for each model/dataset combination. Larger is not always better. This interacts with warmup (need longer warmup for larger batches).

geometric_properties: Loss landscape has many local minima — SGD noise helps navigate between them
Flat minima have large 'basin of attraction' — SGD preferentially finds these
The noise scale η²σ²/|B| determines the 'temperature' of the exploration
statistical_properties: Gradient variance Var(g_B) = σ²/|B| — monitor this to choose batch size
Signal-to-noise ratio: ||∇L|| / √Var(g_B) — should be > 1 for meaningful updates
If SNR >> 1: batch is too large (wasting compute). If SNR << 1: batch too small (updates are noise)
numerical_properties: Batch size must fit in GPU memory: B × n × d × 4 bytes per sample (plus activations)
Gradient accumulation: simulate large batch by accumulating gradients over multiple forward passes
Mixed precision: float16 forward/backward, float32 accumulation reduces memory per sample

enables: TRANSFORMER.TRAINING — warmup interacts with SGD noise at initialization
related: TRANSFORMER.LAYER_NORM — keeps activations where SGD noise is productive

application_relevance: When training custom architectures, batch size is a design decision: too large loses regularization, too small wastes compute. The sweet spot depends on model size and data.
